title: Gradients of the Biases
concept: Biases also receive gradients because they shift a neuron's baseline output; if a neuron is consistently too high or too low, backpropagation corrects the bias as well as the weights.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can a hidden neuron receive more backward blame than another hidden neuron?

options.text: Because it contributed more to the wrong final prediction.
Because it is closer to the input layer.
Because it has a longer variable name.

exercises.explain: Correct! Backprop gives stronger blame to neurons that influenced the error more.

cards.title: When the whole sensor sits too high
cards.body: Imagine a bathroom scale that always starts 3 pounds too high before anyone steps on it. Even if the sensors work perfectly, the baseline is off. A neuron can have the same problem, and that is what the bias helps fix.

cards.title: Biases get gradients too
cards.body: A bias shifts a neuron's output baseline. During backpropagation, the model calculates a gradient for the bias just like it does for weights. If the neuron keeps firing too high or too low overall, the bias must be corrected.

cards.title: A thermostat with the wrong starting offset
cards.body: Suppose your thermostat says the room is warm even when the heater is off. The issue is not the walls or windows; the starting offset is wrong. Adjusting the bias is like fixing that offset so the whole reading shifts into a better position.

cards.title: A face detector that is too eager
cards.body: A camera model keeps predicting 'face' even on plain backgrounds. The connection weights may be part of the problem, but the neuron might also have a bias that pushes its score upward all the time. Backprop can lower that bias so the neuron stops starting from such a high baseline.

cards.title: Learning is not only about connection strengths
cards.body: People often focus only on weights because they connect neurons together. But biases matter too. A network learns both how strongly signals combine (weights) and where each neuron starts from (biases).

cards.title: Weights vs. bias
cards.body: They change different parts of a neuron's behavior:

left.text:
Control how strongly each input matters.
More about connection strength.
'How loud is each voice?'

right.text:
Shifts the neuron's starting level.
More about baseline position.
'Where does the score start?'

exercises.prompt: Why does backpropagation compute gradients for biases?

options.text: Because the bias affects the neuron's baseline output, so it may need correction too.
Because biases store the training dataset inside the neuron.
Because weights cannot ever change during training.
Because bias gradients replace the loss function.

exercises.explain: Correct. If a neuron's baseline is off, the bias must be updated.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a neuron tends to output values that are too high even before its inputs contribute much, its bias may be part of the problem.
exercises.explain: True! That is exactly the kind of issue a bias update can fix.

exercises.sentence: Weights change how inputs combine, while the bias changes the neuron's ___ level.
exercises.explain: The bias sets the starting or baseline level before the weighted inputs fully shape the result.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario suggests that updating a bias could help?

options.text: A neuron keeps producing scores that are too high across many examples, even when the input features are weak.
A laptop battery is low during training.
The dataset folder was moved to another drive.

exercises.explain: Exactly. If the neuron starts too high overall, the bias is a likely thing to adjust.

exercises.prompt: A neuron in a voice model keeps activating too strongly, even on quiet background noise. Engineers discover that even tiny inputs lead to a high score. What is the best explanation for why the bias should receive a gradient update?

options.text: Because the bias may be pushing the neuron's baseline too high, so backprop should correct that offset along with the weights.
Because biases are only used to memorize class names and need to be refreshed.
Because weight updates are impossible unless all biases are frozen.
Because the loss function can only change single numbers, not connections.

exercises.explain: Exactly right. A bias can make a neuron start too high or too low, so it needs its own gradient. Next, we will see why the backward pass must remember values from the earlier forward pass instead of trying to guess them later.
